title: Sorting text
summary: Learn how to sort text columns with ORDER BY so product names and categories appear in a clear, predictable order.

sketchBlocks.title: Sorting text with ORDER BY
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
When you sort text in SQL, the database arranges words in alphabetical order. You do this with `ORDER BY`.

A simple example with the `products` table is:

```sql
SELECT name, category
FROM products
ORDER BY name ASC;
```

Step by step:
- `SELECT name, category` chooses the columns to show.
- `FROM products` says which table to read.
- `ORDER BY name ASC` sorts the rows by the `name` column from A to Z.

With the sample data, names would begin like `Acrylic Paint Set`, `Desk Lamp`, `Fountain Pen`, and so on.

`ASC` means ascending order. For text, that usually means A to Z. If you leave `ASC` out, SQL usually sorts ascending by default.

Try it yourself: write the query in the SQL editor, click Run query, and check how the product names are arranged in the results table.

sketchBlocks.title: Descending text order and multiple sort columns
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
You can also sort text in reverse alphabetical order with `DESC`.

```sql
SELECT name, category
FROM products
ORDER BY name DESC;
```

This sorts product names from Z to A.

You can sort by more than one column too. SQL uses the first sort column first, then uses the next one to break ties.

```sql
SELECT name, category
FROM products
ORDER BY category ASC, name ASC;
```

Step by step:
- First, rows are grouped in alphabetical order by `category`.
- Inside each category, rows are sorted by `name`.

Using the sample data, the `Art` products would appear together, and inside that group `Acrylic Paint Set` would come before `Sketchbook`.

Your turn: change the second query so `category` stays A to Z, but `name` becomes Z to A inside each category. Run it and compare the results table.

quizDraft.title: What ASC means for text
quizDraft.prompt: When you use `ORDER BY name ASC` on a text column, how are the rows sorted?
quizDraft.hint: Think about alphabetical order from the beginning of the alphabet.

help.concept: `ASC` means ascending order. For text values, ascending order is alphabetical from A to Z.
help.hint_1: This question is about how words are arranged, not numbers.
help.hint_2: Imagine a list of names starting with A, then B, then C.

options: From shortest word to longest word
In the order the rows were inserted

quizDraft.title: Second sort column behavior
quizDraft.prompt: In `ORDER BY category ASC, name ASC`, when does SQL use `name` for sorting?
quizDraft.hint: The first column decides the main order. The next column helps inside matching first-column values.

help.concept: With multiple columns in `ORDER BY`, SQL sorts by the first column first. It uses the second column only when rows have the same value in the first column.
help.hint_1: Ask what happens when two products share the same category.
help.hint_2: The second sort does not replace the first one; it breaks ties within it.

options: Only when two or more rows have the same category
Only when two or more rows have the same name

quizDraft.title: Queries that sort text A to Z
quizDraft.prompt: Choose all queries that sort product names in alphabetical order from A to Z.
quizDraft.hint: Look for queries that order by the `name` column in ascending order.

help.concept: Text sorting from A to Z uses `ORDER BY` on the text column with `ASC`, or with ascending order left implicit.
help.hint_1: Check whether the query sorts by `name`, not by `category` or `price`.
help.hint_2: One correct query says `ASC` directly, and another leaves ascending order as the default.

options: SELECT name FROM products ORDER BY name ASC;
SELECT name FROM products ORDER BY name;
SELECT name FROM products ORDER BY name DESC;
SELECT name FROM products ORDER BY category ASC;

quizDraft.title: Valid uses of text sorting
quizDraft.prompt: Choose all statements that correctly describe sorting text in this lesson.
quizDraft.hint: Think about `ASC`, `DESC`, and what happens with more than one sort column.

help.concept: Text columns can be sorted alphabetically with `ASC`, reverse alphabetically with `DESC`, and by multiple columns where later columns break ties from earlier ones.
help.hint_1: Check which statements match alphabetical order and tie-breaking behavior.
help.hint_2: More than one statement is true: one about reverse order and one about multiple columns.

options: `ORDER BY name DESC` sorts text from Z to A.
`ORDER BY category, name` can sort by category first and then by name inside each category.
`ORDER BY` works only with number columns, not text columns.
When sorting text, `DESC` means shortest word first.

quizDraft.title: Build a basic text-sorting query
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange the SQL parts to create a query that shows product names and sorts them from A to Z.
quizDraft.hint: Start with SELECT, then FROM, then the ORDER BY clause.

help.concept: A basic SQL query follows the clause order `SELECT`, `FROM`, then `ORDER BY`.
help.hint_1: The column list comes right after `SELECT`.
help.hint_2: The sorting clause belongs at the end of the query.

quizDraft.title: Build a two-column sort
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange the SQL parts to sort products by category A to Z, then by name Z to A inside each category.
quizDraft.hint: The query still starts with SELECT and FROM. The two sort columns belong together in one ORDER BY clause.

help.concept: Multiple sort columns are written in one `ORDER BY` clause, separated by commas, in the order SQL should apply them.
help.hint_1: Put `category ASC` before `name DESC` because category is the main sort.
help.hint_2: Do not split the sorting into two separate `ORDER BY` clauses.

tokens: ORDER BY category ASC, name DESC;
correctOrder: ORDER BY category ASC, name DESC;

quizDraft.title: Fill the sorting direction
quizDraft.prompt: Complete the query so product names are sorted from Z to A.
quizDraft.hint: Reverse alphabetical order uses the descending keyword.

help.concept: For text columns, `DESC` means descending order, which sorts values from Z to A.
help.hint_1: The blank is the direction keyword after the column name.

quizDraft.template: SELECT name FROM products ORDER BY name [blank1];

quizDraft.title: Fill the second sort column
quizDraft.prompt: Complete the query so rows are sorted by category first, then by product name.
quizDraft.hint: The second sort column should be the text column that stores each product's title.

help.concept: In a multi-column sort, the second column is written after the comma and is used to order rows that share the same first-column value.
help.hint_1: The first sort column is already `category`.
help.hint_2: Use the column that contains values like `Sketchbook` and `Desk Lamp`.

quizDraft.template: SELECT name, category FROM products ORDER BY category ASC, [blank1] ASC;

quizDraft.title: Sort product names alphabetically
quizDraft.prompt: Write a query that shows the `name` and `category` columns from `products`, sorted by `name` from A to Z. Write the query in the SQL editor, click Run query, and check the results table.
quizDraft.hint: Select the two columns, then sort by the text column `name` in ascending order.

help.concept: To sort text alphabetically, use `ORDER BY` with the text column and `ASC`.
help.hint_1: Your query needs `SELECT name, category` and `FROM products`.
help.hint_2: Place the sorting clause at the end and sort by `name`.

quizDraft.starterCode:
SELECT
  name,
  category
FROM products
-- TODO: sort by product name from A to Z
;
quizDraft.solutionCode:
SELECT
  name,
  category
FROM products
ORDER BY name ASC;

quizDraft.title: Sort by category then name
quizDraft.prompt: Write a query that shows `name` and `category` from `products`. Sort first by `category` from A to Z, then by `name` from A to Z inside each category. Write the query in the SQL editor, click Run query, and check the results table.
quizDraft.hint: Use one ORDER BY clause with two columns separated by a comma.

help.concept: A multi-column sort uses one `ORDER BY` clause. SQL applies the first sort column first, then the second one to break ties.
help.hint_1: Both sort columns are text columns in this table.

quizDraft.starterCode:
SELECT
  name,
  category
FROM products
-- TODO: sort by category, then by name
;
quizDraft.solutionCode:
SELECT
  name,
  category
FROM products
ORDER BY category ASC, name ASC;

quizDraft.title: Reverse names inside each category
quizDraft.prompt: Write a query that shows `name` and `category` from `products`. Sort by `category` from A to Z, but sort `name` from Z to A inside each category. Write the query in the SQL editor, click Run query, and compare the grouped results in the results table.
quizDraft.hint: Keep the first sort ascending on `category`, then reverse the second sort on `name`.

help.concept: Each column in a multi-column `ORDER BY` can have its own direction, such as `ASC` for the first column and `DESC` for the second.
help.hint_1: This query still uses only one ORDER BY clause.
help.hint_2: The second sort direction should be the reverse of alphabetical order.

quizDraft.starterCode:
SELECT
  name,
  category
FROM products
-- TODO: category A to Z, then name Z to A
;
quizDraft.solutionCode:
SELECT
  name,
  category
FROM products
ORDER BY category ASC, name DESC;

projectDraft.title: Practice sorting text in product reports
